channel.title: NPR Topics: Education
channel.description: We've been to school. We know how education works. Right? In fact, many aspects of learning — in homes, at schools, at work and elsewhere — are evolving rapidly, along with our understanding of learning. Join us as we explore how learning happens.
channel.copyright: Copyright 2024 NPR - For Personal Use Only

item.title: How to keep an ancient text interesting to students? Make it a summer blockbuster
item.description: English teachers are excited to have a new adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey to use to get students excited about the epic poem.
item.content:encoded:
English teachers are excited to have a new adaptation of Homer's The Odyssey to use to get students excited about the epic poem.

(Image credit: Melinda Sue Gordon/Universal Pic/Melinda Sue Gordon)
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: India's Gen Z 'Cockroach People's Party' started as satire but is serious about change
item.description: They call themselves the "cockroaches" and they're on the march — demanding action against corruption, amid a shortage of opportunities for young people in India.
item.content:encoded:
They call themselves the "cockroaches" and they're on the march — demanding action against corruption, amid a shortage of opportunities for young people in India.

(Image credit: Vipin)
item.dc:creator: Omkar Khandekar

item.title: How the Trump administration's new rules could affect international students
item.description: International students could once stay as long as they needed to complete their college degrees, but that's changing, and the restrictions could cut into college budgets.
item.content:encoded: International students could once stay as long as they needed to complete their college degrees, but that's changing, and the restrictions could cut into college budgets.
item.dc:creator: Elissa Nadworny

item.title: How do young people feel about AI? 7 teens weigh in
item.description: What's it like to grow up and learn in the age of AI? NPR put that question to seven teenagers across the country.
item.content:encoded: What's it like to grow up and learn in the age of AI? NPR put that question to seven teenagers across the country.
item.dc:creator: Lee V. Gaines

item.title: No internet, no screen time? FCC weighs cutting subsidy that lowers school internet bills
item.description: Many schools rely on consumer fees funneled through the federal government to cut internet costs. FCC Chairman Brendan Carr called for ending this program before Donald Trump tapped him for the job.
item.content:encoded:
Many schools rely on consumer fees funneled through the federal government to cut internet costs. FCC Chairman Brendan Carr called for ending this program before Donald Trump tapped him for the job.

(Image credit: Kevin Dietsch)
item.dc:creator: Sequoia Carrillo

item.title: Some Republican states push for new college accreditation agency
item.description: Some Republican states are starting a new college accreditation agency to increase what they call "intellectual diversity".
item.content:encoded: Some Republican states are starting a new college accreditation agency to increase what they call "intellectual diversity".
item.dc:creator: Orlando Montoya

item.title: In private call, Education Dept. tried, but failed, to reassure disability advocates
item.description: The disability community has long worried about what would happen if special education oversight moved from the Education Department to another agency. Now, those moves are becoming more real.
item.content:encoded:
The disability community has long worried about what would happen if special education oversight moved from the Education Department to another agency. Now, those moves are becoming more real.

(Image credit: Kayla Bartkowski/Getty Images)
item.dc:creator: Jonaki Mehta

item.title: Should you sign your kids up for Trump Accounts? Four things to consider
item.description: Families can still benefit even if their children aren't eligible for the free $1,000 contribution from the federal government.
item.content:encoded:
Families can still benefit even if their children aren't eligible for the free $1,000 contribution from the federal government.

(Image credit: TIMOTHY A. CLARY)
item.dc:creator: Stephan Bisaha

item.title: Under a new federal rule, colleges must leave grads better off or lose financial aid
item.description: If an undergraduate program's graduates don't earn more than workers who never went to college, that program could be cut off from federal student loans. But is a degree just about making more money?
item.content:encoded: If an undergraduate program's graduates don't earn more than workers who never went to college, that program could be cut off from federal student loans. But is a degree just about making more money?
item.dc:creator: Tiffany Camhi

item.title: These high school students won NPR's America 250 Student Podcast Challenge
item.description: We asked students around the country to reflect on the phrase "life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness," and recorded what the high schoolers had to say. Note: This piece mentions self harm.
item.content:encoded: We asked students around the country to reflect on the phrase "life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness," and recorded what the high schoolers had to say. Note: This piece mentions self harm.
